title: Complexity Reveals the Microscopic Origins of Macroscopic Dynamics
authors: Haoyang Qian, Beata Casiday, Gabriel Hood, Malbor Asllani
abstract: Real complex systems often exhibit collective transitions emerging from interactions across many components. Classical stability theory describes such transitions in spectral space, where dynamics is organized by spatially extended global eigenmodes whose collective nature obscures direct association with individual physical components. Here, we show that structural disorder in empirical random networks can fundamentally alter this picture. These properties induce spectral localization, causing Laplacian modes to concentrate on small subsets of nodes and producing a mode--node correspondence in which collective dynamics becomes governed predominantly by the local behavior of a dominant node together with their effective coupling to the surrounding network. As a consequence, stability properties can be interpreted directly in node space rather than purely in spectral space. Exploiting this principle, we develop a node-resolved framework that predicts transition onsets, identifies the nodes responsible for emergent collective behavior, and restores interpretability in systems where classical modal theories fail. In heterogeneous reaction networks, the same mechanism gives rise to exotic collective states where different subsets of nodes develop distinct dynamical behaviors beyond those associated with homogeneous assumptions. Our results show that complex network structures naturally generate spectral localization, revealing the microscopic drivers of macroscopic dynamics.
summary: This work introduces a node-resolved framework to analyze collective dynamics in complex networks by exploiting spectral localization in the graph Laplacian. By showing that structural disorder concentrates spectral modes onto specific nodes, the authors allow stability analysis to be performed directly in physical node space rather than abstract spectral space. This restores physical interpretability and predicts the onset and nature of emergent collective behaviors.
main_problem: The paper addresses how to restore physical interpretability to collective dynamics in complex, heterogeneous systems whose stability is traditionally analyzed only in abstract spectral space.
main_result: Structural disorder in networks induces spectral localization, allowing stability properties to be interpreted directly in node space and enabling the identification of specific nodes driving collective transitions.
method: The authors develop a node-resolved framework by exploiting spectral localization of the graph Laplacian eigenvectors to decouple the full system Jacobian into localized, node-dependent components.
model_or_system: The analysis uses general networked systems, exemplified by reaction-diffusion models (like the Brusselator) and SIS dynamics, coupled via the network Laplacian structure.
key_observables: Localized Master Stability Function (MSF), Mean Normalized Eigenvalue Error (MNE), and the identification of dominant nodes responsible for instability.
important_parameters: Network size (N), parameter heterogeneity (s), and the coupling structure (Adjacency vs. Laplacian).
main_assumptions: The core assumption is the existence of strong spectral localization, which allows the complex dynamics to be accurately approximated by local dynamics governed by a 'host node.'
figures_summary: Figures illustrate mode-node mapping via heatmaps of Laplacian eigenvectors, compare MNE across different network types (ER, BA, real-world), and show time series demonstrating various collective states like amplitude chimera and oscillon patterns.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the failure of classical spectral theory to proposing the localization-based node-resolved framework. It then applies this framework to analyze stability, predict transition onsets, and characterize various nonlinear collective states (e.g., chimera states) based on the localized spectrum.
why_interesting: The concept of spectral localization and mapping abstract spectral modes back to physical nodes is a powerful tool for interpreting complex, high-dimensional dynamics, which has parallels in understanding collective excitations in quantum many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Localization-based mode–node mapping reveals node-driven dynamics. (a) Heatmap of the Laplacian eigenvector matrix in absolute value for the International Trade Network of Manufactured Goods [42], showing strong spectral localization. The blue arrow highlights an overlap region where multiple localized modes are associated with the same dominant node. (b) Schematic illustration of the localization reduction: when a Laplacian eigenvector Φ(α) localizes around node η, the action of a diagonal heterogeneous matrix D is approximated by DΦ(α) ≈dηΦ(α), establishing an approximate correspondence between localized spectral modes and dominant physical nodes. (c) Master Stability spectra...

Figure 2. Accuracy of the spectral reduction and relation to eigenvector localization. (a–b) Mean normalized error (MNE) between the full Jacobian JT and its localized approximation b Jα under two structural variations: increasing system size N in Erd˝os–R´enyi (ER) networks (p = 0.2) (a) and increasing the attachment parameter m in Barab´asi–Albert (BA) networks (b). Each point averages over 50 independent connected realizations (self-loops excluded) with node-level heterogeneity in both diffusion and reaction parameters, sampled independently from fixed intervals. The error decreases with system size and connectivity, respectively, with error bars indicating one standard deviation. Insets...

Figure 3. Spectral localization progressively breaks collective synchronization. Each regime is illustrated by the corresponding Master Stability Function (left), representative node trajectories (middle), and the spatial configuration of the network (right). (a–c) Cluster synchronization: the transverse spectrum splits into two groups of exponents, leading to two internally synchronized clusters that remain frequency-locked through network coupling. (d–f) Amplitude chimera: differences in transverse growth rates generate persistent amplitude segregation while phases remain aligned.
